children.name: Maratus volans
children.description: Male displays iridescent fan-like abdominal flap during elaborate courtship dances. Found in a small region of Western Australia; body barely 5mm. Globally known through viral videos from 2011.

children.name: Maratus splendens
children.description: One of the first documented Maratus species; vivid blue-red-white patterning.

children.name: Maratus speciosus
children.description: A peacock spider of coastal Western Australia with bright orange fringes along its abdominal flap. Males raise and wave the fan and third legs in courtship.

children.name: Maratus personatus
children.description: Described in 2015, this tiny peacock spider has a striking blue-and-white facial mask. Endemic to the Cape Le Grand region of Western Australia.

children.name: Maratus harrisi
children.description: A peacock spider from southeastern Australia with a vivid blue-and-red fan. Discovered near Canberra.

children.name: Maratus vesper
children.description: A recently discovered peacock spider from Western Australia with a vivid sunset-coloured abdominal fan. Performs an elaborate courtship dance involving leg-waving and abdominal display.

children.name: Maratus elegans
children.description: A small peacock spider from southwestern Australia with a striking blue-and-orange patterned fan. The male displays to females by raising the fan and third legs and vibrating his colourful abdomen.

children.name: Maratus lineatus
children.description: A peacock spider with bold white stripes on the abdomen fan. Found in Western Australia. Males perform the characteristic courtship fan-fluttering dance on open ground.

children.name: Maratus pavonis
children.description: One of the most brilliantly coloured Maratus species, with an iridescent rainbow-patterned fan that males display during courtship. Found in the Stirling Ranges of Western Australia.

children.name: Portia fimbriata
children.description: Hunts other spiders by mimicking struggling prey in their webs. Plans detours out of sight of prey. The most cognitively complex invertebrate known.

children.name: Portia labiata
children.description: Asian species using the same deceptive web-plucking strategy as P. fimbriata.

children.name: Portia schultzi
children.description: An African araneophagic jumping spider that, like its Asian relatives, invades the webs of other spiders and lures them with deceptive vibratory signals before attacking.

children.name: Portia africana
children.description: A web-invading jumping spider from West and Central Africa. Uses deceptive vibratory signals to lure its spider prey. Shows the same problem-solving capabilities as other Portia species.

children.name: Portia queenslandica
children.description: An Australian araneophagic jumping spider found in the rainforests of Queensland. Known for its cognitive abilities and for taking detours to reach prey spiders from unexpected angles.

children.name: Phidippus regius
children.description: Largest Phidippus; one of the biggest jumping spiders in North America. Males have brilliant iridescent chelicerae. Popular as a pet.

children.name: Phidippus audax
children.description: One of the most common jumping spiders in North America; abundant in gardens and homes.

children.name: Phidippus johnsoni
children.description: A common western North American jumping spider with a bright red abdomen and black-and-white legs. One of the largest jumping spiders in the American West.

children.name: Phidippus clarus
children.description: A red-and-black jumping spider common in old fields across North America, where it hunts among goldenrod and other tall vegetation. Well studied for its courtship and contest behaviour.

children.name: Phidippus otiosus
children.description: A large jumping spider of the southeastern United States, often found on tree trunks and palmetto. Has iridescent green-blue chelicerae.

children.name: Phidippus apacheanus
children.description: A large jumping spider of the southwestern United States and Mexico. The carapace is reddish-orange with females having a black abdomen with white and orange spots.

children.name: Phidippus carneus
children.description: A colourful Phidippus from the southern United States and Mexico. Males have a bright red-orange carapace and iridescent green chelicerae. Found in open scrub and grassland.

children.name: Phidippus bidentatus
children.description: A distinctive Phidippus species with large silvery-white markings on the abdomen. Ranges across the southern United States, from California to Texas.

children.name: Phidippus asprifomis
children.description: A large Phidippus of the southwestern US and Mexico with distinctive shaggy body hair. Males are black with a white stripe on the carapace edge.

children.name: Salticus scenicus
children.description: Familiar spider in Europe including Sweden; seen on sun-warmed walls and window ledges. Black-and-white striped; forward-facing eyes give depth perception to 20cm.

children.name: Salticus cingulatus
children.description: A pale, tree-dwelling relative of the common zebra spider found on sunlit trunks and fences across Europe, including Sweden. Well camouflaged against bark.

children.name: Salticus mutabilis
children.description: A small zebra-patterned jumping spider found across southern Europe and western Asia. Its colouration helps it blend against lichen-covered surfaces where it hunts.

children.name: Salticus scitulus
children.description: A jumping spider found across the Palearctic region. The male has prominent palps and the abdomen is patterned with iridescent scales. Often found on sunlit tree trunks.

children.name: Hyllus giganteus
children.description: Among the largest jumping spiders in Asia; widespread from India to Southeast Asia.

children.name: Hyllus diardi
children.description: A large fuzzy jumping spider of Southeast Asia with prominent forward-facing eyes, often photographed for its expressive 'face'. Hunts insects on foliage.

children.name: Hyllus dilectus
children.description: A large, densely hairy jumping spider from Southeast Asia. Its fuzzy appearance and forward-facing eyes have made it popular in close-up nature photography.

children.name: Hyllus madagascariensis
children.description: The largest jumping spider in Africa, endemic to Madagascar. Reaches over 2 cm in body length. Hunts in broad daylight on the trunks and leaves of large trees.

children.name: Menemerus
children.description: Flattened jumping spiders commonly found on walls and tree trunks in warm regions worldwide.

children.name: Menemerus bivittatus
children.description: A flattened gray jumping spider of tropical and subtropical regions worldwide, common on building walls. Spread widely through human commerce.

children.name: Menemerus semilimbatus
children.description: A flattened, greyish jumping spider found on walls and rocks across the Mediterranean region and into Central Asia. Common around buildings and ruins.

children.name: Plexippus
children.description: Common synanthropic jumping spiders found around human dwellings throughout the tropics and subtropics.

children.name: Plexippus paykulli
children.description: A large jumping spider associated with human buildings throughout the tropics, often hunting insects attracted to lights at night.

children.name: Plexippus petersi
children.description: A tropical jumping spider commonly found around human dwellings from East Africa to Southeast Asia and the Pacific. Males have bold white stripes on a black body.

children.name: Evarcha
children.description: Small ground- and vegetation-dwelling jumping spiders widespread across the Old World.

children.name: Evarcha arcuata
children.description: A small jumping spider of damp heath and grassland across Europe and Asia, including Sweden. Males have tufts of hair on the front legs used in display.

children.name: Evarcha falcata
children.description: A widespread Palearctic jumping spider of heath, bog, and woodland edge, including Sweden. Males are dark with reddish markings.

children.name: Evarcha erratica
children.description: A holarctic jumping spider found in boreal and montane forests. Often seen on conifer branches. The male's front legs bear characteristic dark fringes.

children.name: Evarcha laetabunda
children.description: A common Palearctic jumping spider of open woodland and grassland. Males perform a high-stepping courtship dance with raised front legs.

children.name: Habronattus
children.description: A large North American genus famous for ornate, colourful males and multimodal courtship combining visual displays with substrate-borne vibrations.

children.name: Habronattus pyrrithrix
children.description: A southwestern North American jumping spider whose males display a red face and green legs during vibratory courtship dances. A model for studying colour signalling.

children.name: Habronattus coecatus
children.description: A North American jumping spider in which males perform elaborate dances waving brightly coloured, ornamented legs and faces before females.

children.name: Habronattus americanus
children.description: A colourful western North American jumping spider. Males combine visual displays of brightly coloured legs and faces with species-specific vibrational courtship songs.

children.name: Habronattus dorsalis
children.description: A Habronattus from the western United States. The male is strikingly patterned with a vivid red-orange face and banded legs used in elaborate courtship displays.

children.name: Myrmarachne
children.description: Jumping spiders that mimic ants in body shape and movement, even waving their front legs like antennae, to gain protection and ambush ant prey.

children.name: Myrmarachne formicaria
children.description: A jumping spider that closely mimics an ant in shape and gait, raising its front legs to imitate antennae. Native to Eurasia and recently established in North America.

children.name: Myrmarachne luctuosa
children.description: An ant-mimicking jumping spider from Southeast Asia and Australia. Closely resembles the weaver ant Oecophylla. Its constricted body shape and ant-like movements make it a convincing mimic.

children.name: Myrmarachne plataleoides
children.description: A remarkable ant mimic from Asia, including the first-known example of Batesian mimicry in jumping spiders. It mimics weaver ants with elongated body shape and raised-front-leg antennal imitation.

children.name: Bagheera
children.description: Central American jumping spiders, including the only spider known to be largely herbivorous.

children.name: Bagheera kiplingi
children.description: The only known largely herbivorous spider, feeding mainly on the protein-rich Beltian bodies of acacia plants in Central America. Named for the panther in Kipling's Jungle Book.

children.name: Telamonia
Telamonia dimidiata
children.description: A distinctive jumping spider of Southeast Asia with longitudinal white and red-brown stripes on the abdomen. Once the subject of a famous email hoax claiming it was a deadly 'new species' hiding under toilet seats.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Cosmophasis
Cosmophasis umbratica
children.description: An iridescent green and orange jumping spider from Southeast Asia. The male's body is cloaked in striking metallic green scales. A very fast and visually acute hunter of open foliage.

children.name: Cosmophasis micarioides
children.description: A brilliantly coloured Australian jumping spider with metallic green, blue, and orange bands. Males have a distinctive UV reflectance that plays a role in communication and courtship.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Chrysilla
Chrysilla voluptiosa
children.description: A delicate, long-bodied jumping spider from Southeast Asia with a golden iridescent sheen. Known for its elaborate courtship, the male displays his elongated first pair of legs in a dance.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Saitis
Saitis savignyi
children.description: A jumping spider from the Mediterranean region. Males have a brilliant orange-red patch on the head and perform an energetic courtship dance with raised front legs.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Maevia
Maevia inclemens
children.description: A North American jumping spider famous for having two distinct male morphs — a 'grey' morph and a 'tufted' morph — that use completely different courtship strategies. Females mate with either.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Hasarius
Hasarius adansonii
children.description: A small, cosmopolitan jumping spider of worldwide distribution, likely spread by human commerce. Found in gardens, on walls, and in greenhouses across the tropics and subtropics.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Marpissa
Marpissa muscosa
children.description: One of the largest jumping spiders in Europe, often found on wooden fences, posts, and tree trunks. Well camouflaged against bark. The female stays with her egg sac until spiderlings emerge.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Euophrys
Euophrys frontalis
children.description: A small jumping spider with a distinctive pale yellow 'moustache' marking below the front eyes. Common in sunny spots across Europe, including Sweden, on rocks and walls.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Thorella
Thorella ensigni
children.description: A tiny jumping spider from the eastern United States. Females build distinctive egg sacs lined with silk under loose bark. An active hunter of springtails and other small insects.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Epicadus
Epicadus agilis
children.description: A South American jumping spider known for its bright colours and rapid movements. The carapace often has contrasting patterns of white and red. Hunts in broad daylight on foliage.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Lagnaus
Lagnaus ugueti
children.description: A jumping spider of Sri Lanka and India, known for its subtle patterning that provides camouflage against tree bark. Its taxonomy was only recently resolved.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Pellenes
Pellenes igneus
children.description: A striking jumping spider with a fiery red or orange abdomen. Found in dry, rocky habitats across southern Europe and Central Asia. Males establish territories on sun-exposed rocks.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Aelurillus
Aelurillus v-lunatus
children.description: A small, ground-dwelling jumping spider with a distinctive V-shaped white mark on the abdomen. Common in sandy and rocky habitats across Europe, including Sweden.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Carrhotus
Carrhotus viduus
children.description: A deep velvety black jumping spider with white markings around the anterior eyes. Found in low vegetation across India and Southeast Asia.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Baryphymus
Baryphymus luigii
children.description: A jumping spider found in the highlands of New Guinea. Known for its cryptic colouration that mimics the lichen-covered bark of its native trees.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Zebra
Zebra devasa
children.description: A large zebra-patterned jumping spider from Southeast Asia. Despite the genus name 'Zebra', it is not closely related to the European Salticus zebra spiders but shares similar bold stripes.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Mogius
Mogius tesselatus
children.description: A small jumping spider of moist woodlands, named for its mosaic-like pattern of light and dark scales. Found in leaf litter and on low shrubs across Europe.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Simonaethus
Simonaethus seriatus
children.description: A jumping spider of rice paddies and wetlands in Southeast Asia. Well studied for its role as a natural pest controller in agricultural ecosystems.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Haplocyclus
Haplocyclus albomaculatus
children.description: A ground-dwelling jumping spider that prefers dry, sandy soils across Central and Eastern Europe. Its white-spotted pattern provides excellent camouflage on sandy terrain.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Thiodina
Thiodina boiensis
children.description: A slender, tree-bark-dwelling jumping spider of the southeastern United States. Its elongated green body makes it almost invisible on vegetation. Hunts small insects and other spiders.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Paraplexippus
Paraplexippus lucium
children.description: An African jumping spider that favours savannah woodlands. Named for its striking glossy black and white colour pattern. Hunts on the trunks of baobab and acacia trees.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Henziella
Henziella pulchella
children.description: A delicate and attractive jumping spider from the forests of Borneo and Sumatra. Its carapace has iridescent green and blue tones. An agile hunter in the forest understorey.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Nannia
Nannia arabica
children.description: A jumping spider of the Arabian Peninsula and East Africa. Adapted to arid and semi-arid environments. Its pale sandy colouration serves as camouflage against desert substrates.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Rhaenia
Rhaenia crystalli
children.description: A green iridescent jumping spider from the Andean cloud forests of Ecuador and Colombia. Its body shimmers with crystalline reflections that help disrupt the outlines of its body against leaves.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Viciria
Viciria mustelina
children.description: A slender-bodied jumping spider from Southeast Asia that hunts on herbaceous plants. Its elongated carapace and agile movements make it an effective hunter in dense vegetation.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Abundia
Abundia lepida
children.description: A small, brightly coloured jumping spider from the Atlantic Forest of Brazil. Its common name refers to its extremely active hunting style, constantly turning its body and head before pouncing.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Ampicius
Ampicius amoenus
children.description: A Neotropical jumping spider found in lowland rainforests. Males have iridescent blue-green markings on the carapace. Hunts on the upper surfaces of broad leaves.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.

children.name: Simonia
Simonia aurata
children.description: A golden-haired jumping spider from the savannahs of East Africa. Its golden reflective hairs help it regulate temperature while hunting in direct sunlight on termite mounds and rocks.
A genus of jumping spiders with distinct visual characteristics.
